title: Slow-cooker chicken cacciatore
subtitle: Italian hunter's chicken with tomatoes and olives
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised in a rich tomato sauce with olives, capers, garlic, and rosemary until deeply flavourful and falling from the bone. A slow-cooker version of the Italian hunting dish. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Pollo alla cacciatora (hunter's chicken) is a traditional Italian braise of chicken with tomatoes, wine, herbs, and whatever the hunter had to hand — olives, capers, mushrooms, or anchovies depending on the region. Documented by Marcella Hazan in The Classic Italian Cook Book (1973) and by Anna Del Conte in Gastronomy of Italy (1987). The slow cooker is a natural fit for the dish, which improves with time and low heat.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Refrigerate and reheat gently.

content.text: Browning the chicken before it goes into the slow cooker is the step that separates a pale, boiled result from a rich, braised one. A frying pan at high heat takes 10 minutes and builds the colour and fond that become the foundation of the sauce. The olives and capers go in at the end of cooking so they keep their texture and don't turn bitter.

Season the chicken with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over high heat. Brown the thighs skin-side down for 4–5 minutes until deep golden. Turn and brown the underside for 2 minutes. Transfer to the slow cooker. In the same pan, fry the {{onion}} of chopped onion over medium heat for 4 minutes. Add {{garlic}} of garlic; stir for 1 minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes and bring to a simmer. Pour over the chicken in the slow cooker. Add the rosemary sprigs. Cover and cook on LOW for 5–6 hours or HIGH for 3–4 hours until the chicken is tender and coming away from the bone. In the last 30 minutes, stir in the olives and capers. Remove the rosemary stems and taste for seasoning before serving with polenta or pasta.

items.cause: Slow cooker releases moisture from the chicken without the evaporation of an open pan.
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 30 minutes and cook on HIGH. Alternatively, transfer the sauce to a saucepan and reduce for 10 minutes before returning to the slow cooker.
Add the olives and capers in the final 30 minutes only. Long cooking makes them sour and metallic.

items.cause: Braised chicken skin is always soft — the slow cooker produces moist heat, not dry.
items.fix: If crisp skin is important, place the slow-cooked thighs skin-side up under a hot grill for 3–4 minutes before serving.

content.text: Cacciatore varies significantly by region. The Roman version uses white wine and no tomatoes. The northern versions lean on mushrooms and butter. The Tuscan versions add anchovies. The version here is broadly Sicilian in character, with olives, capers, and tomatoes. None is more correct than another — the dish is defined by what the hunter found, not by a fixed canon.
